title: DreamCoder-Style Library Learning for Operator Discovery
subtitle: How wake-sleep loops can discover reusable semantic primitives — from program synthesis to Schankian operators

children.children: DreamCoder (Ellis et al., 2021) revolutionized program synthesis with a simple but profound insight: the best primitives aren't given — they're DISCOVERED through iterative compression of successful programs.

This connects directly to our Schankian operator project. Instead of hand-coding primitives like ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, we can LEARN them from event data using the same wake-sleep architecture that discovers programming primitives.

DreamCoder discovers reusable PROGRAM fragments. We want to discover reusable SEMANTIC fragments. Both are compression problems — finding the minimal set of building blocks that can compose into the full range of observed phenomena.
DreamCoder alternates between two phases in a wake-sleep loop. Understanding this architecture is crucial because we'll adapt it for operator discovery.

items.definition: Given tasks (input-output examples), search for programs that solve them using the current library. This is synthesis — combining primitives to explain observations.
Analyze successful programs from the wake phase. Extract common subexpressions and add them to the library as new primitives. This is abstraction — finding reusable patterns.
The growing vocabulary of primitives. Starts with basic operations (+, -, if, map). Grows to include discovered abstractions (fold-with-plus, filter-even, etc.).
Minimum Description Length. The library should minimize: (library size) + (total program lengths using that library). This balances having useful primitives vs. library bloat.

children.children: MDL is the secret sauce. Too few primitives → programs are long and convoluted. Too many primitives → the library is bloated with rarely-used operations. MDL finds the sweet spot automatically.

Mathematically: minimize L(Library) + Σ L(Program_i | Library), where L is description length in bits. Adding a primitive costs bits (larger library) but saves bits if many programs use it (shorter programs).
This connects to Kolmogorov complexity — the theoretical minimum description length. True primitives are those that can't be further compressed; they're the incompressible atoms of the domain.
Let's visualize how DreamCoder discovers primitives. In this demo, we'll watch the wake-sleep loop discover common patterns from a simple domain: list manipulation.

props.title: Wake-Sleep Library Learning
props.description: Watch primitives emerge from compression pressure

children.children: Notice how after a few iterations, the system discovers 'map-with-multiply' as a useful abstraction — it appears in multiple solutions and compresses the overall description length.

Now here's the key insight: event descriptions are programs too. 'John gave Mary the book' is a 'program' that transforms world states. The operator ATRANS is a reusable 'function' that appears across many such programs.

children.bridgeText: The same compression algorithm works in both domains. The 'programs' are different (functions vs. state transforms) but the learning principle is identical.

children.children: Imagine training on event descriptions with before/after entity states:

props.code:
Event: 'John gave Mary the book'
Before: John.has=[book], Mary.has=[]
After:  John.has=[], Mary.has=[book]

Event: 'Alice sold Bob the car'
Before: Alice.has=[car], Bob.has=[]
After:  Alice.has=[], Bob.has=[car]

Event: 'The thief stole the painting'
Before: Museum.has=[painting], Thief.has=[]
After:  Museum.has=[], Thief.has=[painting]

children.children: All three events share the same state transformation pattern: possession transfers from source to destination. A wake-sleep loop would discover this as a reusable primitive — ATRANS — that compresses all three descriptions.
Let's formalize how DreamCoder's architecture maps to operator discovery. This gives us a concrete training procedure.

steps.title: Initialize Library
steps.description: Start with basic transforms: IDENTITY (no change), NEGATE (flip sign), SCALE (multiply). These are the '+' and '-' of state space.

steps.title: Wake Phase: Explain Events
steps.description: For each event in training data, search for operator compositions that transform the before-state into the after-state. Record successful 'programs' (operator sequences).

steps.title: Sleep Phase: Extract Patterns
steps.description: Analyze successful operator sequences. Find common sub-sequences. If [OP_A, OP_B, OP_C] appears frequently, add it as new primitive OP_ABC.

steps.title: Compress Library (MDL)
steps.description: Remove primitives that don't pay for themselves. A primitive is worth keeping only if it saves more bits in program lengths than it costs in library size.

steps.title: Iterate
steps.description: Repeat wake-sleep cycles. Library evolves toward the optimal set of primitives for the domain.

children.children: DreamCoder uses symbolic search (enumeration) in the wake phase. This is exact but slow. For operators, we can use neural search: train a neural network to predict likely operator sequences for an event.

The neural 'recognition model' learns to amortize search: instead of enumerating all possibilities, it directly predicts high-probability operator compositions. This is exactly how the sleep phase trains the recognition model in DreamCoder.

For Schankian operators: the recognition model takes an event description (text) and outputs a probability distribution over operator compositions. This is essentially a structured prediction problem with a learned vocabulary.
Let's simulate the operator discovery process. Feed in event descriptions and watch the system learn to decompose them into reusable primitives.

props.title: Discover Operators from Events
props.description: Watch ATRANS, PTRANS, and MTRANS emerge from event data

children.children: After several iterations, the system discovers three stable primitives: one for possession transfer (ATRANS), one for physical movement (PTRANS), and one for information transfer (MTRANS). These are exactly Schank's classic primitives — but discovered, not hand-coded!
You might ask: why not just cluster verb embeddings? Why the elaborate wake-sleep machinery? The answer is COMPOSITIONALITY.
Consider the event: 'John mailed Mary the package.' This involves BOTH physical movement (package travels through postal system) AND possession transfer (John no longer has it, Mary will).

Pure clustering would put 'mail' in ONE cluster — either transfer or movement. But library learning discovers that 'mail' = PTRANS ∘ ATRANS (composition). It's not a single primitive; it's a compound operation.
This compositionality is why library learning is more powerful. It discovers not just primitives but how they COMBINE — which is essential for understanding complex events.

Library learning discovers: (1) atomic primitives, (2) common compositions, (3) how to decompose novel events. Clustering only gives you (1). The compositional structure is what makes operators actually useful for reasoning.

DreamCoder's 'dreaming' phase (part of sleep) is particularly clever: it generates SYNTHETIC tasks that exercise the newly discovered primitives. This prevents forgetting and ensures the library generalizes.

For Schankian operators, dreaming would generate synthetic events: 'What if we applied ATRANS to these entities?' → generate plausible event description → train recognition model to recover the operator.

This is essentially DATA AUGMENTATION guided by the learned primitives. If we've discovered ATRANS, we can generate infinite training examples of possession transfer by composing ATRANS with different entities.

The dreaming phase also tests compositional generalization: 'Can the system understand PTRANS ∘ ATRANS even if it never saw that exact combination in training?' If not, the primitives aren't truly compositional.

This connects to human dreaming, which consolidates memories by replaying and recombining experiences. The analogy isn't just poetic — it's functional. Both human and machine dreaming serve to extract abstract patterns from specific experiences.

questions.question: What happens during the WAKE phase of DreamCoder?
options: New primitives are added to the library
The system searches for programs to solve tasks
Common subexpressions are extracted
The library is compressed via MDL
questions.explanation: The wake phase is about SYNTHESIS — using the current library to find programs that solve the given tasks. Abstraction and library modification happen in the sleep phase.

questions.question: Why does MDL (Minimum Description Length) prevent library bloat?
options: It limits the number of primitives to a fixed constant
It only adds primitives that are used more than 10 times
It balances library size against total program length savings
It removes all primitives after each iteration
questions.explanation: MDL is an economic trade-off: adding a primitive costs bits (larger library) but saves bits if many programs use it. A primitive only survives if the savings exceed the cost. This automatically finds the right number of primitives.

questions.question: What advantage does library learning have over pure clustering for operators?
options: It's faster to compute
It requires less training data
It discovers compositional structure, not just categories
It produces more clusters
questions.explanation: Library learning discovers how primitives COMPOSE — that 'mail' = PTRANS ∘ ATRANS. Clustering would just put 'mail' in one category, missing the compositional structure that's essential for understanding complex events.

children.children: How does DreamCoder-style learning fit with our rotation matrix operators? Beautifully, it turns out.

steps.title: Operators as Matrix Functions
steps.description: Each primitive operator is a learnable rotation matrix W_op. The library is a set of these matrices plus rules for composing them (matrix multiplication).

steps.title: Programs as Matrix Products
steps.description: An event 'program' is a product of operator matrices: W_event = W_op1 × W_op2 × ... The result transforms the before-state embedding to the after-state embedding.

steps.title: Discovery via Factorization
steps.description: The sleep phase finds common matrix factors across event programs. If W_A × W_B appears in many events, we add W_AB = W_A × W_B as a new primitive.

steps.title: MDL on Matrix Parameters
steps.description: Library size = number of parameters in all operator matrices. Program length = number of operator applications. MDL finds the right trade-off.

children.children: This bridges our geometric (rotation matrix) framework with the symbolic (discrete primitive) tradition. Operators are BOTH learnable continuous transforms AND discrete compositional units. DreamCoder's library learning gives us the algorithm to discover them.
Moving from theory to practice requires addressing several challenges.

items.definition: What are the 'before' and 'after' states? We need embeddings of entity states that capture relevant properties (possession, location, knowledge, etc.). LLM embeddings are a starting point; domain-specific encodings may work better.
The space of operator compositions is exponentially large. Need efficient search: beam search, learned proposal distributions, or constrained enumeration.
Operators have different numbers of arguments (ATRANS has 3: giver, receiver, object; PTRANS has 2: mover, destination). The library must handle variable arity.
How do we know the discovered operators are 'right'? Comparison to Schank's hand-coded primitives. Cross-linguistic universality. Downstream task performance (event prediction, QA).

children.children: A key technical challenge: operators have different numbers of arguments. PTRANS(person, destination) has 2. ATRANS(giver, receiver, object) has 3. How do we handle this in a uniform framework?
Solution 1: Fixed maximum arity with NULL slots. All operators take 4 arguments; unused slots get null embedding. Simple but wasteful.
Solution 2: Concatenate all participant embeddings, regardless of count. Operator matrices adapt their size based on input. More flexible but requires careful architecture.
Solution 3: Graph neural network approach. Each participant is a node; the operator is a message-passing function. Most elegant but highest complexity.

questions.question: In our framework, what does an 'operator program' look like?
options: A sequence of text tokens
A product of rotation matrices
A list of verb lemmas
A set of entity embeddings
questions.explanation: An event 'program' is a composition of operator matrices: W_event = W_op1 × W_op2 × ... This product transforms the before-state to the after-state.

questions.question: What does 'dreaming' accomplish in operator learning?
options: It reduces memory usage
It speeds up the wake phase
It generates synthetic events to test and reinforce primitives
It removes unused operators from the library
questions.explanation: Dreaming generates synthetic events by composing discovered operators. This tests compositional generalization and provides additional training data for the recognition model.

questions.question: How would we detect that 'mail' = PTRANS ∘ ATRANS?
options: By looking up 'mail' in a dictionary
By clustering 'mail' with similar verbs
By finding that the composition best explains 'mail' events via MDL
By asking GPT-4 to decompose it
questions.explanation: Library learning would discover that decomposing 'mail' as PTRANS ∘ ATRANS yields a shorter total description (via MDL) than treating 'mail' as a separate primitive. The composition is preferred because it reuses existing primitives.

children.children: DreamCoder isn't the only approach to library learning. Several related ideas connect to our operator discovery goal.

items.definition: Language-guided program synthesis. Uses natural language hints to guide the search. For operators: event descriptions themselves are the language hints.
Recent work uses LLMs to propose candidate programs, DreamCoder-style to refine. For operators: LLMs generate candidate decompositions, wake-sleep validates and abstracts.
Discovers hierarchical structure in sequences. Operators form a 'grammar' of event descriptions. Grammar induction methods could discover operator composition rules.
Learns logical rules from examples. Could learn operator definitions as logical predicates: ATRANS(X,Y,Z) :- has(X,Z,before), has(Y,Z,after).

children.children: Modern LLMs might already encode operator-like structure. Instead of learning from scratch, we could use DreamCoder-style abstraction on LLM-generated event decompositions. The LLM provides the search; the library learning provides the compression.

items.definition: We don't need to hand-code Schankian primitives. Wake-sleep library learning can discover them from event data through compression pressure.
Minimum Description Length automatically finds the right number and type of primitives. True primitives are those that can't be further compressed.
Library learning discovers how operators COMBINE, not just what they are. This compositional structure is what makes operators useful for reasoning about novel events.
Synthetic event generation tests and reinforces compositional abilities. The system learns to handle combinations it's never seen.
Operators are both learnable rotation matrices (continuous, geometric) and discrete compositional primitives (symbolic, combinatorial). DreamCoder-style learning unifies both views.

children.children: The DreamCoder approach transforms operator discovery from a linguistic taxonomy problem into a compression and abstraction problem. This is more principled (MDL gives clear objectives), more scalable (wake-sleep is iterative), and more likely to discover genuinely universal primitives.
To go deeper into the ideas covered in this tutorial:

items.definition: Ellis et al. (2021) 'DreamCoder: Bootstrapping Inductive Program Synthesis with Wake-Sleep Library Learning' — the foundational paper.
Wong et al. (2021) 'Leveraging Language to Learn Program Abstractions and Search Heuristics' — language-guided extension.
Our deeper dive into using Minimum Description Length specifically for finding the optimal operator set.
Related compression-based approach: bottleneck the information flow to discover essential semantic content.
